content.problemStatement: In this problem you are given a sequence of NNN positive integers S[1],S[2],…,S[N]S[1],S[2],\dots,S[N]S[1],S[2],…,S[N]. In addition you are given an integer TTT, and your aim  is to find the number of quadruples (i,j,k,l)(i,j,k,l)(i,j,k,l), such that 1≤i<j<k<l≤N1 \le i < j < k < l \le N1≤i<j<k<l≤N, and S[i]+S[j]+S[k]+S[l]=TS[i] + S[j] + S[k] + S[l] = TS[i]+S[j]+S[k]+S[l]=T.  That is, the number of ways of picking four numbers from the sequence summing up to TTT.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains N+2 space-separated integers. The first two integers are N and T. The next N integers are S[1], S[2], ..., S[N].
content.outputFormat: A single integer which is the number of valid quadruples.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The quadruple (1, 4, 5, 6) is valid because S[1] + S[4] + S[5] + S[6] = 3 + 2 + 5 + 10 = 20.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a brute force approach with four nested loops to generate all possible quadruples. However, this approach would have a time complexity of O(N^4), which is inefficient for large inputs. A more efficient approach is to use a two-pointer technique with two nested loops to fix two elements and then use the two-pointer technique to find the remaining two elements that sum up to the target value.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^3) — we use three nested loops to generate all possible quadruples and then use the two-pointer technique to find the remaining two elements
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — we only use a constant amount of space to store the input and the count of quadruples
